threat.title: Protected Health Information Exfiltration from Networked Medical Devices
threat.description: An attacker intercepts or extracts Protected Health Information (PHI) and Personal Health Data (PHD) from medical devices that transmit patient data over hospital networks without adequate encryption. Medical devices commonly transmit patient vitals, diagnostic results, medication data, and device telemetry. Many legacy protocols (HL7v2, DICOM without TLS) transmit data in cleartext. This threat spans HIPAA (US), GDPR (EU), and medical device regulations, making it a multi-jurisdictional compliance issue.

versions_affected: All networked medical devices transmitting PHI/PHD

attack.scenario: 1. Attacker gains access to the hospital network segment where medical devices communicate. 2. Attacker performs passive network sniffing on HL7v2, DICOM, or proprietary protocols. 3. Attacker extracts patient data including names, dates of birth, medical record numbers, diagnostic results, and medication information. 4. Data is exfiltrated for sale on dark markets or used for identity theft, insurance fraud, or targeted attacks on individuals.

cve_references.description: Philips e-Alert patient monitoring - cleartext transmission of patient data and credentials
Contec CMS8000 patient monitor - data exfiltration over unencrypted channels to hardcoded IP

real_world_breaches.name: MedStar Health Breach (2016)
real_world_breaches.description: Ransomware attack on hospital network affected medical device operations and exposed patient data. Highlighted interconnection risks between IT and medical device networks.

security_research.title: IMDRF N60: Principles and Practices for Medical Device Cybersecurity - Data Protection Requirements

mitigations.title: Encrypt All PHI/PHD in Transit
mitigations.description: Implement TLS 1.2+ for all data transmissions containing patient data. For legacy protocols (HL7v2, DICOM), use TLS wrappers or VPN tunnels. Mandate DICOM TLS (DICOM Supplement 178) and HL7 FHIR over HTTPS.

mitigations.title: Encrypt PHI/PHD at Rest
mitigations.description: Implement AES-256 encryption for all patient data stored on the medical device, including local caches, logs, and configuration files. Use hardware-backed key management for encryption keys.

mitigations.title: Network Segmentation for Medical Devices
mitigations.description: Deploy medical devices on isolated VLANs with firewall rules restricting communication to only authorized endpoints (clinical systems, update servers). Implement micro-segmentation to prevent lateral movement.

mitigations.title: Data Minimization and Anonymization
mitigations.description: Configure medical devices to transmit only the minimum necessary patient data. Where possible, use de-identified or pseudonymized data for device telemetry and analytics. Implement automatic data purging after clinical need expires.
